content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

September 20, 1976

Roger Duerksen
State Capitol, Room 4047
Sacramento, CA  95814

Dear Mr. Duerksen:

     This letter is to confirm the advice provided to
you by Jeanne Pritchard on Friday, September 10, 1976. The
facts as we understand them are as follows:  From March to
June of this year, Senator Russell's office received approxi-
mately 700 letters from persons urging him to support the
budget appropriation for the AIRB. Senator Russell did not
want to respond to the letters until he could determine
whether certain changes in the law could be made which would
make his support of the appropriation possible. Eventually
those changes were made, the Senator supported the appropri-
ation, and in July the Governor signed it. Due to the press
of business, the Senator's office did not prepare a form
letter response to those persons who had written concerning
the appropriation until early September. Since Senator
Russell is seeking reelection, you inquired of the Commission
staff as to whether these letters were prohibited from being
sent at public expense by Government Code section 89001.

     Because your letters were already addressed and
dated, it was impractical to delay the mailing until after
the Commission could consider the issue in its October meet-
ing. Accordingly, the staff gave you informal advice which
represented the staff's conclusions. This advice was that
the mailing in question was not prohibited by Section 89001.
However, it also was made clear to you that the facts which
you presented posed a close question and that it is entirely
possible the Commission would have reached a different con-
clusion had it considered the issue. Accordingly, although
acting pursuant to the staff's advice will immunize the
Senator from any enforcement proceeding by the Fair Political
Practices Commission based on the letter in question, the
advice has no precedential value with respect to future
cases. In addition, you were informed that the letters must

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 · EXECUTIVE/LEGAL 322-5901 · ENFORCEMENT/CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441

The image appears to be mostly blank/white with only some very faint marks or artifacts visible at the top and right edges of the page. The marks are too faint or unclear to transcribe with certainty.

Roger Duerksen
September 20, 1976
Page Two


be "in response" to the letters which you received. Thus,
their content must be limited to a discussion of the issues
surrounding the budget appropriation for the AIRB.

     If you have any further questions, please do not
hesitate to contact me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Ted Prim
                                        Staff Counsel
                                        Legal Division

TP:plh

I can see this image appears to be mostly blank or very faintly printed. There are what look like two small curved marks near the top of the page and possibly some very faint text or marks in the upper right corner and lower left corner, but they are too illegible to transcribe accurately.

[illegible]

embedding.qa_text: This letter is from the Fair Political Practices Commission of the State of California, dated September 20, 1976. It is addressed to Roger Duerksen at the State Capitol, Room 4047, Sacramento, CA 95814. The letter confirms advice provided by Jeanne Pritchard on Friday, September 10, 1976. The facts as understood are as follows: From March to June of that year, Senator Russell's office received approximately 700 letters urging him to support the budget appropriation for the ALRB (Alcoholic Beverage Control Board). Senator Russell did not respond to these letters until he could determine whether certain changes in the law could be made which would make his support of the appropriation possible. Eventually, those changes were made, and in July the Governor signed it. Due to the press of business, the Senator's office did not prepare a form letter response to those persons who had written concerning the appropriation until early September. Since Senator Russell is seeking reelection, you inquired of the Commission staff as to whether these letters were prohibited from being sent at public expense by Government Code Section 89001. Because your letters were already addressed and dated, it was impractical to delay the mailing until after the Commission could consider the issue in its October meeting. Accordingly, the staff gave you informal advice which represented the staff's conclusions. This advice was that the mailing in question was not prohibited by Section 89001. However, it also was made clear to you that the facts which you presented posed a close question and that it is entirely possible the Commission would have reached a different conclusion had it considered the issue. Accordingly, although acting pursuant to the staff's advice will immunize the Senator from any enforcement proceeding by the Fair Political Practices Commission based on the letter in question, the advice has no precedential value with respect to future cases. In addition, you were informed that the letters must The image you provided is mostly blank, with very faint and mostly illegible text in the upper right corner. The majority of the content cannot be reliably transcribed or described due to its extremely low quality and lack of clear detail. If there is any specific part of the image that you would like me to focus on or if you have another image, please provide it for better results. be "in response" to the letters which you received. Thus, their content must be limited to a discussion of the issues surrounding the budget appropriation for the ALRB. If you have any further questions, please do not hesitate to contact me. Sincerely, Ted Prim Staff Counsel Legal Division TP: plh The image you provided is mostly blank, with only two small circles visible near the top center of the page. There is no text or significant content to transcribe or analyze further. If you have another page or another section of this document that you would like me to help with, please provide it.
